text.title: Policy as Code: OPA, Kyverno, and Admission Control Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed in real deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Flip to Enforce on Monday morning; all teams' workloads blocked; incident bridge opens; trust in security tooling collapses for a quarter.
antipattern.root-cause: Engineer assumed 'baseline policy is simple, nothing will break'; no audit phase, no remediation.
antipattern.fix: Roll back to Audit. Review the violation log. Remediate existing resources (PR each). Then flip to Enforce per-namespace gradually.

antipattern.symptom: Policy YAML applied to cluster; uses a wrong fieldRef; silently fails open — does nothing — for weeks before anyone notices.
antipattern.root-cause: Engineer skipped kyverno test; relied on visual inspection.
antipattern.fix: Add kyverno test fixtures (PASS + FAIL per rule). Block PR merge on test-failure. Add a smoke ApplyPolicy fixture in CI that asserts the policy actually rejects the FAIL resource.

antipattern.symptom: Operator kubectl-applies a one-off exemption to fix prod fire; commit forgotten; cluster + repo diverge; next ArgoCD sync re-enforces and breaks the workaround.
antipattern.root-cause: Out-of-band fix during an incident bypasses GitOps.
antipattern.fix: Hot-fix exemptions go through a fast-track PR + ArgoCD sync, never via kubectl. If GitOps controller is slow, fix the controller — do not bypass it.

antipattern.symptom: Trusted-registry policy enforces ghcr.io/acme/* allowlist; verifyImages absent; CI compromised → pushes malicious image to the right registry → admission accepts it.
antipattern.root-cause: Allowlist gives false sense of security; signature check skipped.
antipattern.fix: Pair trusted-registries with verifyImages cosign keyless OIDC. Tie issuer + subject regexes to the CI's own workflow path.
